content:
Compaction is the most-run, least-examined operation in agent engineering. Here’s what it actually deletes, why, and how to measure it yourself.

7 min read
Just now

--

Hour three of a long session. The agent that confidently described your architecture an hour ago now asks which file the config lives in. It suggests code that contradicts what it wrote earlier. It quietly stops honoring a rule you set in the first five minutes.
The usual diagnosis is that the model degraded, or that “context rot” set in, as if entropy just happens to a conversation. Both are wrong, and the wrongness matters, because the real explanation is mechanical and therefore fixable.
Nothing degraded. A codec ran.
Compaction is compression, and compression is a theory of what matters
When the context window approaches its limit, the agent doesn’t crash and it doesn’t forget in any biological sense. It runs a memory manager. The conversation history gets summarized, the original messages are discarded, and the loop continues from the summary. Everything after that point is reasoning over a compressed artifact rather than the thing itself.
That’s a lossy codec. And here is where the comparison gets uncomfortable.
JPEG is lossy too. But JPEG’s loss function is specified. It discards high-frequency detail because human vision is measurably less sensitive to it, and that tradeoff lives in explicit quantization tables that someone designed, tuned, and published. You can set a quality dial and know precisely what you’re trading. The loss is engineered.
Compaction has no such specification. It’s a language model summarizing a language model’s context, and what it decides to keep is emergent rather than designed. There’s no quantization table. There’s no published curve describing what gets sacrificed first as pressure increases. The loss profile is a behavior, not a spec.
Which means every long-running agent is applying an unexamined theory of importance to its own reasoning, thousands of times, and almost nobody has checked what that theory actually is.
The proof that it’s unspecified: the experts disagree
Read enough practitioner writing on this and you find something telling. One careful analysis of a leading coding agent’s compaction states flatly that user instructions are never lost, that the summary has a sacred section reserved for them. Another equally careful writeup documents the opposite: a session where security rules from the initial prompt vanished after compression, and the agent started approving requests it had been told to block. A third reports a known bug where project configuration content isn’t reliably preserved in compaction summaries at all.
These people are not sloppy. They’re all describing real observations. The disagreement is the finding.
Nobody argues about what JPEG discards. It’s in the specification. The fact that serious engineers reach opposite conclusions about what survives compaction is direct evidence that the loss function isn’t written down anywhere, including, apparently, inside the system running it.
What actually survives, and why it has nothing to do with importance
Here’s the mechanic that reframes everything, and it’s the part most people get backwards.
Things that survive compaction largely don’t survive because the summarizer judged them important. They survive because they were never inside the codec’s reach to begin with.
The system prompt survives, because it isn’t part of the message history being summarized. Project-root instruction files survive, because they get re-injected from disk after the compaction runs. They come back not because the summary preserved them but because something outside the loop reloaded them.
Now look at what disappears. Path-scoped rules and nested instruction files, gone until something re-reads a matching file. The contents of files the agent read mid-session, gone. The reasoning behind decisions made two hours ago, gone, unless it happened to make the cut.
So survival is not a function of importance. It’s a function of location. Whether a piece of state lives inside the compressible region or outside it. That’s an architectural property you control, and most people are leaving it to chance.
The Chesterton’s fence problem
Look at the shape of what gets kept versus what gets shed and a pattern emerges that should worry you.
Compaction reliably preserves what the loop is doing. Current task. Recent errors. File names. Active state. It sheds why the loop is doing it that way. The architectural decision survives as a fact; the three constraints that forced it do not. The rule survives as a line; the reason it exists does not.
Chesterton’s fence is the principle that you shouldn’t tear down a fence until you know why it was built. Compaction systematically deletes exactly that: it keeps the fence and discards the reason. Which leaves the agent standing in a field of constraints it can no longer justify, and it will do one of two bad things. It will tear one down, because nothing in its remaining context explains why it’s there. Or it will obey it rigidly in a situation where the original reason wouldn’t have applied, because it can’t reason about a rationale it no longer has.
Both failures look like the model got dumber. Neither is.
Generation loss
Now compound it. The second compaction doesn’t operate on your original conversation. It operates on the summary produced by the first one. The third operates on a summary of a summary.
This is generation loss, and it’s the exact failure mode of re-encoding a JPEG repeatedly. Each pass is individually defensible and the composition is not. Detail goes first. Then the context that made the remaining detail meaningful. By the third cycle you’re several lossy transforms removed from what actually happened, and the agent has no way to know it, because a compressed artifact doesn’t carry a signal saying how compressed it is.
There’s a related detail worth knowing, because it explains why compaction so often fires at the worst moment. The summarization itself costs tokens, so the system reserves headroom to run it. On a 200K window, compaction triggers around 167K of actual usage, leaving roughly 33K for the codec to do its work. That buffer isn’t idle space. It’s the compressor’s workspace.
The consequence: auto-compaction fires while you’re deep in a task, precisely when the model is juggling its most fragile intermediate state, because that’s when the window fills. The moment of maximum context pressure is the moment of maximum loss.
Measure your own loss function
You don’t have to accept an unspecified codec. You can characterize it. Here’s a probe that treats compaction as the black box it is and measures what falls out.
The method: construct a session containing tagged content of known categories, force a compaction, then check which categories survived into the summary.
# compaction_probe.py
# Characterize what your agent's compaction actually preserves.
# Method: plant tagged canaries of different types, compact, count survivors.CANARIES = {
"explicit_rule": "RULE-7F2A: never write to /etc without confirmation.",
"decision": "DECISION-9C1B: auth handled at middleware, not per-route.",
"rationale": "REASON-9C1B: per-route auth was rejected because the "
"audit log needs a single choke point.",
"style_pref": "STYLE-3E88: no trailing commas in generated configs.",
"tool_output": "OUTPUT-5D44: build succeeded, 0 warnings.",
"file_content": "FILE-1A03: config.yaml contains db.pool_size = 12.",
"abandoned_path": "DEAD-8B77: tried Redis for sessions, rejected, too slow.",
}def build_session(canaries, filler_turns):
"""Plant each canary early, then bury it under enough filler
to force the window past the compaction threshold."""
msgs = [{"role": "user", "content": text} for text in canaries.values()]
msgs += filler_turns # real tool calls / file reads, not lorem ipsum
return msgsdef survivors(summary_text, canaries):
"""A canary survived only if its ID is still recoverable."""
return {
name: (tag.split(":")[0] in summary_text)
for name, tag in canaries.items()
}# Run the session until compaction fires, capture the post-compaction
# context, then:
#
# results = survivors(post_compaction_context, CANARIES)
#
# Run it 20 times. The survival rate per category IS your loss function.
# That table is the quantization table nobody published.Two things make this honest rather than decorative. The filler has to be real work, actual tool calls and file reads, because a summarizer treats genuine task content differently than padding. And you have to run it repeatedly, because the loss profile is stochastic. A category that survives 19 times out of 20 is a different engineering fact than one that survives 11 times out of 20, and you cannot tell them apart from a single run.
What you get at the end is a table: survival probability by content type. That table is the specification the codec never came with, measured rather than assumed, for your workload rather than someone’s blog post.
The design principle that falls out of this
Once you accept that survival is about location rather than importance, the mitigation stops being a tip and becomes an architecture.
Anything load-bearing must live outside the compressible region. Not “stated clearly at the top of the conversation,” because the conversation is exactly what gets compressed. Load-bearing means it lives in the system prompt, or in a file the agent re-reads, or in structured state the harness re-injects on every turn. If a rule matters enough that its loss would be a bug, it does not belong in the message history. That’s not a preference. It’s a consequence of where the codec’s boundary sits.
The corollary: compact deliberately, at task boundaries, rather than letting the window decide. After a merge, after a debugging path resolves, before switching from research to implementation. At those moments the intermediate state genuinely is disposable, and you’re choosing what to discard while you still know what it was. Wait for auto-compaction and you’ve handed that choice to a codec, at the exact moment you have the most to lose.
The thing underneath
Every summary is a claim about what mattered. That’s true of meeting minutes, of a handover note, of an institution remembering its own decisions long after everyone who understood the reasoning has left. What survives isn’t what was most important. It’s what the summarizer’s implicit model of importance happened to select for, and that model is almost never examined, because summarizing feels like a neutral act of compression rather than an act of judgment.
Your agent runs that act of judgment on its own reasoning, automatically, at the moment of maximum pressure, with a loss function nobody wrote down.
You can keep treating that as weather. Or you can measure it, and then move everything that matters out of its reach.
